source.title: BSEE Accident Investigation Report - Structural Damage to Crane/Lifting Equipment

event.top_event: Loss of load control - riser joint dropped
actions_taken: Revised Work Instruction Manual (WIM) for running riser and BOPs to designate a single person to engage secondary manual lock
Added secondary verifier to verification process
Revised Job Safety Analysis (JSA) to include secondary verifier and painted indicator rods red and green for visibility
Submitted revisions to personnel zone management procedures for drill floor area
event.summary: On January 9, 2019 at 0220 hours, a 35,000 pound slick riser joint fell across the rig floor of the drillship Noble Don Taylor while being hoisted using a hydraulic riser running tool (RRT) suspended from the top drive. The RRT was not properly seated in the riser and the secondary manual lock was not engaged. A crew member narrowly avoided being struck. The riser joint crushed the drops shed. No injuries were reported. Estimated damage was $184,000.
recommendations: Implement designated single person responsibility for engaging secondary manual lock on RRT
Establish secondary verifier for lock engagement confirmation
Improve visual identification of indicator rods with color coding (red and green)
Review and revise personnel zone management procedures for drill floor operations

hazards.name: Pressurized riser system with suspended load
Heavy lifting operations on drill floor

threats.name: RRT not properly seated in riser joint
Secondary manual lock not engaged on RRT
Ambiguous communication regarding lock status between crew members
Subsea Engineer unfamiliar with RRT locking procedure

consequences.name: Dropped riser joint on drill floor
Near miss - personnel nearly struck by falling load

controls.name: Pre-job safety meeting and Work Instruction Manual (WIM)
controls.barrier_role: Administrative control to ensure crew awareness and planning

supporting_text: Prior to commencing work, the 'A' drill crew which consisted of 2 Drillers, 2 Assistant Drillers, 7 Floorhands and 1 Subsea Engineer held a pre job safety meeting. A Work Instruction Manual (WIM) report was generated with all the necessary personnel, permits, tools, equipment and Job Safety Analysis (JSA's). All documents were reviewed and signed by the crew.

controls.name: Primary hydraulic lock on RRT
controls.barrier_role: Engineering control to mechanically secure riser joint

supporting_text: If the RRT is not properly seated in the riser, the primary lock can still be engaged even though the dogs are not engaged in the grooves of the riser. If the dogs are not engaged in the grooves of the riser, the RRT will not have an effective hold on the riser, and the secondary manual lock cannot be engaged.
the investigation team identified the primary cause of this incident to be that the RRT was not properly seated in the riser and the secondary manual lock was not engaged.

controls.name: Secondary manual lock on RRT with indicator pins
controls.barrier_role: Engineering control to verify primary lock engagement
controls.lod_basis: Secondary verification mechanism for primary lock

supporting_text: To function the manual lock, the operator must pull a 'T-Handle' up and rotate the manual lock ring 10 degrees. The 'T-Handle is then released and it engages into a hole on the tool.
The inability to function the secondary manual lock would be a sign that the RRT is not properly seated in the riser and not safe to lift.

The Subsea Engineer stated that he engaged the manual lock, but when the RRT was lowered to the rig floor, it was found in the open position. The secondary manual lock could not have opened on its own, indicating that it was never actually locked.

controls.name: Designated crew member verification and thumbs-up communication
controls.barrier_role: Administrative control for lock status confirmation

supporting_text: The designated crew member, a Floorhand, communicated to the Driller via a thumbs up that both locks were engaged and ready to be lifted.

The Floorhand that had made the first function was the designated person to close the manual lock of the RRT. When the RRT was stabbed into the second riser joint, he was placing a water hose in the choke and kill lines. While doing this, he saw the Subsea Engineer manipulating the secondary manual lock on the RRT. He asked another crew member if the Subsea Engineer locked the RRT, and he replied yes and gave him a thumbs up.
The Subsea Engineer stated that he was on the rig floor checking the seals in the riser, and he asked another Floorhand if he was going to lock the RRT, and he responded with, 'You got it.'

controls.name: RRT manual documentation and warning labels

supporting_text: In the RRT's manual under the 'Manual Lock-Out' section, it states: 'Ensure the running tool is locked properly to the riser joint before picking up. Failure to heed this warning may cause serious injury or death to personnel.'

controls.name: Rig Manager final inspection before lowering RRT
controls.barrier_role: Administrative control to detect lock failure before load drop

supporting_text: The Rig Manager instructed the rig to not lower the RRT until he was on the rig floor, and he stated that when the RRT was lowered, he observed the manual lock was not engaged.

controls.name: Crew member situational awareness and escape
controls.barrier_role: Human factor control to avoid personnel injury

supporting_text: There was a crew member located near the drops shed, but he saw the RRT slip out of the riser and he ran in a safe direction.

The Floorhand that had positioned himself near the auxiliary drops shed saw the RRT slip out of the riser joint, and ran in the opposite direction. Moments after he cleared the area, the riser joint impacted the drops shed and crushed it beyond repair.

notes.rules: JSON output only. mentioned fields must be evidence-based. Use null for unknown values.
